NIE’28 ECE
8/3/2026
~ Tanish
: Dear Students Greetings of the Day. The Department of Electronics & Communication Engineering is organizing a 5-day Skill Development Program (SDP) on MATLAB for the IV Semester students from March 9 to March 13, 2026, in collaboration with the MathWorks team. This program aims to provide you with hands-on exposure to MATLAB, a widely used technical computing and simulation tool in academia, research, and industry. The sessions will be aligned with your current courses such as Signals & Systems, Linear Integrated Circuits, and Digital System Design, enabling you to better understand how theoretical concepts can be analyzed and visualized using modern engineering tools. From an undergraduate learning perspective, this SDP will help you: Visualize and analyze engineering concepts through simulations. Strengthen your understanding of signal processing, circuit analysis, and digital system design. Gain practical exposure that complements your classroom learning. From a career and employability perspective, proficiency in MATLAB is highly valued in areas such as electronics, communication systems, signal processing, embedded systems, and data analysis. Learning this tool during your undergraduate program can help you: Develop industry-relevant technical skills. Improve your problem-solving and analytical abilities using simulation tools. Enhance your profile for internships, higher studies, and placements, as many technology companies and research organizations use MATLAB for modeling and system analysis. Day 1 Plan (9:00 AM – 11:00 AM): Students will create their MATLAB accounts and complete the installation of MATLAB on their personal laptops as well as on the PCs allotted in the Skill Lab with guidance from the resource persons. Important Evaluation Component: On the first four days, there will be a graded activity conducted every evening, and students will receive marks based on their performance. Students will also work on a mini project in each track during the program. The mini projects will be evaluated on the final day (afternoon session). The marks obtained through these activities and projects will be counted towards the Experiential Learning component for the courses Signals & Systems, Linear Integrated Circuits, and Digital System Design. Seating Arrangement: Students with personal laptops may attend the sessions in Radhakrishna Hall. In case Radhakrishna Hall reaches full capacity, students may proceed to the Skill Lab, 2nd Floor, Admin Block. Students without laptops should directly report to the Skill Lab, where systems will be available for use. All students are advised to be punctual, bring their laptops if available, and actively participate in the sessions to make the best use of this opportunity.

area reset, data, readonly     export __Vectors __Vectors     dcd 0; initialization of stack memory     dcd Reset_Handler     area mycode, code, readonly     entry     export Reset_Handler Reset_Handler     ldr r0,=0x12345678 ; load operation     ldr r1,=0xabcdef01     ldr r2,=0xffffffff     ldr r3,=0x87654321     ldr r4,=0x01fedcba     mov r5,r0 ; data transfer     mov r6,r1     mov r7,r2     mov r8,r3     mov r9,r4 stop b stop     end

#include <Wire.h> #include <Adafruit_MCP4725.h> Adafruit_MCP4725 dac; // Pins const int pot1Pin = A0; const int pot2Pin = A1; // Thresholds & Rules const int deviationThreshold = 10;       // 10% maximum deviation (Rule E.2.6.4) const unsigned long errorTimeLimit = 100; // 100ms time limit (Rule E.2.6.5) const float minValidVoltage = 0.5;       // Minimum valid voltage (Rule E.2.6.10) const float maxValidVoltage = 4.5;       // Maximum valid voltage (Rule E.2.6.10) // Timing and State unsigned long errorStartTime = 0; bool errorActive = false; bool powerCut = false; // Latches the motor cutoff void setup() {   Serial.begin(115200); // Faster baud rate for less delay   Serial.println("APPS Safety Controller Initialized");   // Init DAC (ADDR pin tied to GND -> 0x60)   if (!dac.begin(0x60)) {     Serial.println("MCP4725 not found. Check wiring!");     while (1);   } } void loop() {   // 1. Read Raw Values   int pot1Value = analogRead(pot1Pin);   int pot2Value = analogRead(pot2Pin);   // 2. Calculate Raw Voltages   float pot1Voltage = pot1Value * (5.0 / 1023.0);   float pot2Voltage = pot2Value * (5.0 / 1023.0);   // 3. Convert to Percentage AND Constrain to 0-100%   // constrain() prevents negative numbers or values > 100%   int pot1Percent = constrain(map(pot1Value, 70, 410, 0, 100), 0, 100);   int pot2Percent = constrain(map(pot2Value, 154, 819, 0, 100), 0, 100);   // 4. Evaluate Safety Rules (Implausibility Conditions)   bool outOfRange = (pot1Voltage < minValidVoltage || pot1Voltage > maxValidVoltage ||                       pot2Voltage < minValidVoltage || pot2Voltage > maxValidVoltage);                         bool deviationTooHigh = (abs(pot1Percent - pot2Percent) > deviationThreshold);   bool isImplausible = outOfRange || deviationTooHigh;   // 5. Handle the 100ms Rule   if (isImplausible) {     if (!errorActive) {       errorStartTime = millis(); // Start the timer       errorActive = true;     }          // If implausibility persists for >= 100ms, cut power     if (millis() - errorStartTime >= errorTimeLimit) {       powerCut = true;      }   } else {     // Sensors agree and are in range. Reset timer.     errorActive = false;          // Optional but highly recommended: require pedal to be lifted to < 5% to reset the power cut     if (powerCut && pot1Percent < 5) {         powerCut = false;      }   }   // 6. Actuate DAC (Throttle Output)   if (powerCut) {     // HARD CUT TO 0V     dac.setVoltage(0, false);     Serial.println("ERROR: Motor Power Cut! (Deviation or Out of Range > 100ms)");   } else {     // Normal Operation     uint16_t dacValue = map(pot1Percent, 0, 100, 0, 3276);     dac.setVoltage(dacValue, false);          // Print telemetry occasionally to avoid slowing down the safety loop     if (millis() % 100 < 10) {        Serial.print("P1: "); Serial.print(pot1Percent); Serial.print("%  |  ");       Serial.print("P2: "); Serial.print(pot2Percent); Serial.println("%");     }   }   // Removed delay(50). The loop should run as fast as possible to meet the 100ms rule accurately.   delay(2); // Tiny delay just to stabilize ADC reads }

# Source Degenerated Common Source Amplifier (TSMC 180nm) --- # Introduction Analog CMOS amplifiers are fundamental building blocks in many electronic systems such as signal processing circuits, sensors, and communication systems. Among various amplifier configurations, the *common source amplifier* is widely used because it provides good voltage gain and relatively simple implementation. In order to improve linearity and stabilize the operating point, *source degeneration* is introduced. Source degeneration introduces local negative feedback through a source transistor or resistor, which improves stability and reduces distortion. In this experiment, a *Source Degenerated Common Source MOS Amplifier* is designed using *TSMC 180 nm CMOS technology*. The …

## Bottom NMOS (M3) The lower NMOS transistor establishes the required bias current for the cascode stage. | Parameter | Calculation | Value | |-----------|-------------|-------| | VGS3 | VTHn + Vov | 0.36 + 0.25 = *0.61 V* | | Gate Voltage | Bias value | *0.61 V* | | Source Voltage | Ground | *0 V* | | Drain Voltage | VS1 | *0.284 V* | | VDS3 | VD3 − VS3 | *0.284 V* | ### Saturation Verification | Condition | Result | |----------|--------| | VDS3 ≥ Vov | *0.284 ≥ 0.25 * | Thus *M3 operates in saturation* and provides the required current source. --- ## Cascode NMOS (M1) The cascode transistor improves the output resistance and increases the gain of the amplifier. | Parameter | Calculation | Value | |-----------|-------------|-------| | Gate Voltag…

# Conclusion The AC analysis of the designed CMOS amplifier was successfully performed. The voltage gain obtained from simulation is *Av ≈ 2.811* and the -3 dB bandwidth is *6.6069 MHz*. Using these values, the Gain Bandwidth Product (GBP) is calculated as *18.571 MHz, which is close to the simulated **Unity Gain Bandwidth (UGB) ≈ 17.742 MHz*. This confirms that the circuit is functioning correctly and the simulation results match the theoretical expectations.9:16 pm
9:16 pm
--- # Inference From the performed analyses, the *Source Degenerated Common Source Amplifier* operates correctly under the given design specifications. The DC operating point confirms that all transistors remain in the *saturation region*, ensuring proper amplifier functionality. Transient analysis shows that the output signal has a larger amplitude than the input signal, verifying that the circuit provides *voltage amplification. The practical gain obtained from simulation is approximately **2.81 V/V (8.49 dB)*. AC analysis indicates that the amplifier operates over a *wide bandwidth in the MHz range. It is also observed that the **Unity Gain Bandwidth (UGB) is approximately equal to the Gain Bandwidth Product (GBP)*, which is consistent with theoretica…

Among various amplifier configurations, the *common source amplifier* is widely used because it provides good voltage gain and relatively simple implementation. In order to improve linearity and stabilize the operating point, *source degeneration* is introduced. Source degeneration introduces local negative feedback through a source transistor or resistor, which improves stability and reduces distortion.
